candidates.title: Cyanobacterial blooms
candidates.org_or_authors: Huisman J, Codd GA, Paerl HW, Ibelings BW, Verspagen JMH, Visser PM
candidates.why_relevant: The single most authoritative, comprehensive modern review of cyanobacterial bloom biology, species traits (buoyancy, N-fixation, toxin production), and the environmental drivers of bloom formation and expansion — a foundational citation for this lane.

candidates.title: Climate change: a catalyst for global expansion of harmful cyanobacterial blooms
candidates.why_relevant: Seminal, highly-cited paper establishing the mechanistic links between warming, stratification, low wind mixing, and buoyant cyanobacteria dominance — core bloom-ecology theory underpinning most later work.

candidates.title: Controlling harmful cyanobacterial blooms in a climatically more extreme world: management options and research needs
candidates.source_type: Peer-reviewed review article (Journal of Plankton Research)
candidates.why_relevant: Reviews bloom ecology under climate extremes (storms, droughts) and connects biological mechanisms (nutrient pulses, stratification) to bloom persistence, complementing the buoyancy/succession angle.

candidates.title: Non-Nitrogen-Fixers or Nitrogen-Fixers? Factors Distinguishing the Dominance of Chroococcal and Diazotrophic Cyanobacterial Species
candidates.why_relevant: Directly addresses the ecological/physiological factors that determine whether N-fixing taxa (Dolichospermum, Aphanizomenon) or non-fixers (Microcystis) dominate a bloom — core succession biology.

candidates.title: The role of heterocytes in the physiology and ecology of bloom-forming harmful cyanobacteria
candidates.why_relevant: Focused review on heterocyst structure/function and nitrogen fixation specifically in the context of bloom-forming diazotrophic genera (Dolichospermum, Aphanizomenon) — directly matches the lane's N-fixation focus.

candidates.title: Transcriptionally active nitrogen fixation and biosynthesis of diverse secondary metabolites by Dolichospermum and Aphanizomenon-like Cyanobacteria in western Lake Erie Microcystis blooms
candidates.why_relevant: Empirical evidence of active N-fixation by co-occurring diazotrophic taxa within a Microcystis-dominated bloom, illustrating multi-taxon bloom community dynamics and succession relevant to Lake Erie (a well-studied US case).

candidates.title: Rapid flotation of Microcystis wesenbergii mediated by high light exposure: implications for surface scum formation and cyanobacterial species succession
candidates.why_relevant: Mechanistic study of light-driven buoyancy regulation and surface scum formation with direct implications for species succession among Microcystis morphospecies — core to buoyancy/scum focus of this lane.

candidates.title: Extracellular polysaccharide synthesis in a bloom-forming strain of Microcystis aeruginosa: implications for colonization and buoyancy
candidates.why_relevant: Details EPS-mediated colony formation as a buoyancy-regulation mechanism in Microcystis, complementing gas-vesicle-based explanations of bloom flotation.

candidates.title: pH-dependent gas vesicle formation in Microcystis
candidates.why_relevant: Mechanistic detail on gas vesicle protein gene regulation by pH, a key environmental control on Microcystis buoyancy — directly supports the gas-vesicle mechanism in the lane's scope.

candidates.title: Selection of photosynthetic traits by turbulent mixing governs formation of cyanobacterial blooms in shallow eutrophic lakes
candidates.why_relevant: Recent mechanistic work connecting turbulent mixing regimes to trait selection and surface bloom/scum formation, extending Huisman's classic mixing-based bloom theory.

candidates.title: The role of surface water waves on cyanobacterial blooms in lakes
candidates.org_or_authors: Gushulak et al., Limnology and Oceanography Letters
candidates.why_relevant: Very recent (2025) study examining wave/wind physical processes controlling surface scum aggregation, updating the physical-ecological understanding of scum formation.

candidates.title: Recent Advances in the Ecology of Bloom-Forming Raphidiopsis (Cylindrospermopsis) raciborskii: Expansion in China, Intraspecific Heterogeneity and Critical Factors for Invasion
candidates.org_or_authors: International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health (MDPI) / PMC
candidates.why_relevant: Comprehensive, recent review of Cylindrospermopsis/Raphidiopsis raciborskii bloom ecology, invasive range expansion, and the traits (heterocysts, akinetes, phenotypic plasticity) driving its global spread.

candidates.title: The ecology of the invasive cyanobacterium Cylindrospermopsis raciborskii (Nostocales, Cyanophyta) in two hypereutrophic lakes dominated by Planktothrix agardhii (Oscillatoriales, Cyanophyta)
candidates.why_relevant: Directly studies competitive/successional dynamics between two named taxa in the lane's scope (Cylindrospermopsis and Planktothrix) in real hypereutrophic lakes.

candidates.title: The PEG-model of seasonal succession of planktonic events in fresh waters
candidates.org_or_authors: Sommer U, Gliwicz ZM, Lampert W, Duncan A (foundational); reviewed/updated by Sommer et al.
candidates.why_relevant: The classic, foundational model of seasonal phytoplankton succession in lakes (spring diatoms to summer cyanobacteria) — the theoretical backbone for understanding bloom initiation timing relative to season.

candidates.title: Learn about Harmful Algae, Cyanobacteria and Cyanotoxins
candidates.why_relevant: Authoritative federal overview identifying the dominant U.S. bloom-forming genera (Microcystis, Dolichospermum, Planktothrix, etc.) and basic bloom biology, useful as a citable baseline/taxonomy reference.

candidates.title: Field and laboratory guide to freshwater cyanobacteria harmful algal blooms for Native American and Alaska Native communities (USGS Open-File Report 2015-1164)
candidates.org_or_authors: Rosen BH, St. Amand A; U.S. Geological Survey
candidates.source_type: Federal agency field guide (USGS Open-File Report)
candidates.why_relevant: Federal identification guide with field/microscopic imagery and biology of the dominant bloom-forming genera named in this lane, useful as an authoritative taxonomic/identification reference.

candidates.title: Toxic Cyanobacteria in Water, 2nd Edition — Chapter 4: Understanding the occurrence of cyanobacteria and cyanotoxins
candidates.why_relevant: WHO's authoritative technical monograph chapter synthesizing global science on cyanobacterial bloom occurrence, buoyancy, nutrient drivers and taxa — a globally recognized reference standard for bloom biology.

candidates.title: Eutrophication of Lakes Cannot Be Controlled by Reducing Nitrogen Input: Results of a 37-Year Whole-Ecosystem Experiment
candidates.org_or_authors: Schindler, D.W., Hecky, R.E., Findlay, D.L., et al.
candidates.source_type: Peer-reviewed journal article (PNAS) — whole-lake manipulation experiment
candidates.why_relevant: Foundational 37-year Experimental Lakes Area study establishing the phosphorus-control paradigm and showing N-fixing cyanobacteria can compensate for N reduction, directly framing the P-vs-N debate.

candidates.title: Controlling Eutrophication: Nitrogen and Phosphorus
candidates.org_or_authors: Conley, D.J., Paerl, H.W., Howarth, R.W., Boesch, D.F., Seitzinger, S.P., Havens, K.E., Lancelot, C., Likens, G.E.
candidates.why_relevant: The landmark rebuttal/synthesis paper that reframed the debate toward dual N+P reduction; essential counterpoint to the Schindler phosphorus-only paradigm and the canonical citation for this controversy.

candidates.title: Schindler's legacy: from eutrophic lakes to the phosphorus utilization strategies of cyanobacteria
candidates.org_or_authors: Authors per FEMS Microbiology Reviews (Oxford Academic)
candidates.why_relevant: Recent review tracing the phosphorus paradigm's history and physiological mechanisms of cyanobacterial P-utilization, useful for updating the classic debate with modern mechanistic understanding.

candidates.title: Controlling harmful cyanobacterial blooms in a world experiencing anthropogenic and climatic-induced change
candidates.org_or_authors: Paerl, H.W., Huisman, J. (or Paerl & Paul, per Paerl Lab)
candidates.why_relevant: Major Paerl-lab review of how nutrients, temperature, stratification, and hydrology interact to drive cyanoHABs, and why dual nutrient control is increasingly necessary under climate change.

candidates.title: Controlling harmful cyanobacterial blooms in a climatically more extreme world: management options and research needs
candidates.org_or_authors: Paerl, H.W., Otten, T.G., Kudela, R.
candidates.source_type: Peer-reviewed journal article (Journal of Plankton Research)
candidates.why_relevant: Synthesizes synergistic drivers (nutrients, light, temperature, stratification, residence time, biotic interactions) of cyanoHABs and articulates current dual-nutrient management thinking.

candidates.title: Dual nitrogen and phosphorus reductions are needed for long-term mitigation of eutrophication and harmful cyanobacterial blooms in the hydrologically-variable San Francisco Bay Delta, CA
candidates.source_type: Peer-reviewed journal article (Science of the Total Environment)
candidates.why_relevant: Recent U.S. case study quantifying dual N+P reduction targets under variable hydrology, directly engaging the co-limitation/dual-nutrient-reduction controversy with a defensible U.S. system.

candidates.title: Dual phosphorus and nitrogen nutrient reduction will be more effective than a phosphorus-only reduction in mitigating diatom and cyanobacterial blooms in Lake Erie, USA-Canada
candidates.why_relevant: Lake Erie modeling study directly quantifying (e.g., ~40% dual reduction) the comparative efficacy of P-only vs. dual N+P reduction — a flagship U.S. binational system central to this debate.

candidates.title: Roles of Nutrient Limitation on Western Lake Erie CyanoHAB Toxin Production
candidates.why_relevant: Documents N and P co-limitation of both biomass and toxin (microcystin/anatoxin) production in Lake Erie, linking nutrient limitation directly to toxicity outcomes relevant to risk/treatment framing.

candidates.title: Predicting Cyanobacteria dominance in lakes
candidates.org_or_authors: Downing, J.A., Watson, S.B., McCauley, E.
candidates.source_type: Peer-reviewed journal article (Canadian Journal of Fisheries and Aquatic Sciences)
candidates.why_relevant: Widely-cited empirical model relating total P, total N, N:P ratio, and temperature to cyanobacterial dominance probability across many lakes — a core quantitative driver-threshold reference.

candidates.title: Cylindrospermopsis raciborskii dominates under very low and high nitrogen-to-phosphorus ratios
candidates.why_relevant: Challenges the simple 'low N:P favors cyanobacteria' rule by showing a bimodal (both low and high N:P) dominance pattern for an invasive toxic cyanobacterium, nuancing the N:P threshold debate.

candidates.title: Nitrogen Limitation of Intense and Toxic Cyanobacteria Blooms in Lakes within Two of the Most Visited Parks in the USA: The Lake in Central Park and Prospect Park Lake
candidates.why_relevant: U.S. urban lake case evidence for nitrogen (not phosphorus) as the proximate limiting nutrient controlling toxic bloom intensity, illustrating site-specific departures from the classic P-paradigm.

candidates.title: Future Projections of Water Temperature and Thermal Stratification in Connecticut Reservoirs and Possible Implications for Cyanobacteria
candidates.source_type: Peer-reviewed journal article (Water Resources Research, AGU)
candidates.why_relevant: U.S. reservoir modeling of projected warming/stratification changes and their implications for cyanobacteria, directly connecting thermal stratification driver to future cyanoHAB risk.

candidates.title: Increasingly severe cyanobacterial blooms and deep water hypoxia coincide with warming water temperatures in reservoirs
candidates.why_relevant: Empirical long-term U.S. reservoir evidence linking rising temperatures directly to bloom severity and stratification-driven hypoxia, reinforcing temperature as a key co-driver alongside nutrients.

candidates.title: Impacts of Climate Change on the Occurrence of Harmful Algal Blooms
candidates.why_relevant: Authoritative U.S. federal synthesis of how warming, altered precipitation/hydrology, and nutrient loading interact as climate-mediated drivers of HAB occurrence — a citable federal-tier framing source.

candidates.title: Reducing the water residence time is inadequate to limit the algal proliferation in eutrophic lakes
candidates.source_type: Peer-reviewed journal article (Journal of Environmental Management)
candidates.why_relevant: Evidence-based caution on hydrologic-control (flushing/residence time) limits for bloom mitigation, important for honestly bounding claims about residence time as a manageable driver.

candidates.title: EPA Drinking Water Health Advisories for Cyanotoxins
candidates.why_relevant: Central EPA hub summarizing health advisory levels for both microcystins and cylindrospermopsin in drinking water, with links to underlying toxicology reports.

candidates.title: Recommendations for Cyanobacteria and Cyanotoxins in Recreational Waters (EPA 823-R-19-001)
candidates.why_relevant: Provides EPA's recommended recreational water thresholds for microcystins and cylindrospermopsin (health-based swimming advisory levels), directly usable for risk-tier definitions in a HAB tool.

candidates.title: Toxic Cyanobacteria in Water: A Guide to Their Public Health Consequences, Monitoring and Management (2nd edition)
candidates.why_relevant: The foundational international reference on cyanotoxin health consequences; defines WHO's tiered probability-of-adverse-effect thresholds (cell counts, chlorophyll-a) used globally, including by many US states.

candidates.title: Cyanobacterial toxins: microcystins — Background document for development of WHO Guidelines for drinking-water quality and Guidelines for safe recreational water environments (WHO/HEP/ECH/WSH/2020.6)
candidates.why_relevant: WHO's updated provisional guideline value derivation for microcystin-LR in drinking water (1 µg/L) with full toxicological dose-response justification.

candidates.title: Interplay of Nutrients, Temperature, and Competition of Native and Alien Cyanobacteria Species Growth and Cyanotoxin Production in Temperate Lakes
candidates.why_relevant: Directly quantifies how nutrient concentration and temperature interact to drive cyanotoxin production in temperate lakes — a key mechanistic input for a treatment/risk model's driver analysis.

candidates.title: Hot and toxic: Temperature regulates microcystin release from cyanobacteria
candidates.org_or_authors: Peer-reviewed authors, Science of the Total Environment
candidates.why_relevant: Establishes a specific temperature-dependent mechanism for microcystin release, supporting temperature as a leading feature/driver in a HAB toxin-risk model.

candidates.title: Impact of Environmental Factors on the Regulation of Cyanotoxin Production
candidates.why_relevant: Review synthesizing nutrient, light, and temperature triggers of cyanotoxin production across genera — useful background for justifying which environmental covariates matter.

candidates.title: On the Chemistry, Toxicology and Genetics of the Cyanobacterial Toxins, Microcystin, Nodularin, Saxitoxin and Cylindrospermopsin
candidates.why_relevant: Comprehensive peer-reviewed review covering chemistry and toxicology of all four major cyanotoxin classes including saxitoxin and cylindrospermopsin, filling coverage beyond microcystin-only sources.

candidates.title: Toxic Effects Produced by Anatoxin-a under Laboratory Conditions: A Review
candidates.why_relevant: Dedicated review of anatoxin-a toxicology; notes WHO considers the anatoxin-a database inadequate for a formal guideline value, an important honest limitation for the toxin's risk framing.

candidates.title: Canine Cyanotoxin Poisonings in the United States (1920s–2012): Review of Suspected and Confirmed Cases from Three Data Sources
candidates.why_relevant: Peer-reviewed historical dataset of 368 documented US canine cyanotoxin poisoning cases across 231 bloom events, with fatality attribution by toxin type (anatoxin vs. microcystin) — concrete real-world exposure/consequence data.

candidates.title: About the One Health Harmful Algal Bloom System (OHHABS)
candidates.why_relevant: The only national U.S. public health surveillance system tracking HAB events and associated human/animal illness; a live, citable data source for exposure/health-outcome case counts by state and year.

candidates.title: Surveillance for Harmful Algal Bloom Events and Associated Human and Animal Illnesses — One Health Harmful Algal Bloom System, United States, 2016–2018
candidates.why_relevant: Reports concrete national counts (421 HAB events, 389 human illness cases, 413 animal illness cases, 2016-2018) directly usable as a real-data baseline for exposure/illness incidence.

candidates.title: Harmful Algal Bloom–Associated Illnesses in Humans and Dogs Identified Through a Pilot Surveillance System — New York, 2015
candidates.org_or_authors: CDC / NY State Dept. of Health authors
candidates.why_relevant: State-level pilot linking documented human and dog illness cases to specific bloom events, useful as a regional case-study example of the exposure pathway.

candidates.title: Widespread global increase in intense lake phytoplankton blooms since the 1980s
candidates.org_or_authors: Ho, J.C., Michalak, A.M., Pahlevan, N.
candidates.why_relevant: Seminal 30-year, 71-lake, 33-country Landsat-based global study showing peak bloom intensity increased in >2/3 of lakes; the foundational quantitative evidence for a long-term climate-linked trend, with NASA/USGS/Carnegie companion coverage.

candidates.title: Concerns about phytoplankton bloom trends in global lakes
candidates.source_type: Peer-reviewed journal article (Nature, Matters Arising / critique)
candidates.why_relevant: Direct scientific critique of the Ho/Michalak/Pahlevan 2019 global trend claim, raising methodological concerns; essential for representing genuine debate/uncertainty in attribution rather than presenting the trend as settled.

candidates.title: Perceived global increase in algal blooms is attributable to intensified monitoring and emerging bloom impacts
candidates.org_or_authors: Hallegraeff, G.M., Anderson, D.M., Belin, C., et al.
candidates.source_type: Peer-reviewed journal article (Nature Communications Earth & Environment)
candidates.why_relevant: Global HAB-event-database analysis finding no uniform trend once adjusted for monitoring effort - the central counter-narrative/uncertainty piece explicitly framing the climate-attribution debate this lane requires.

candidates.title: Climate change and the aquatic continuum: A cyanobacterial comeback story
candidates.why_relevant: Reviews mechanisms by which warming and extreme wet/dry oscillations favor cyanobacterial dominance across the aquatic continuum, directly addressing the 'warming favors cyanobacteria' mechanism.

candidates.title: Climate Change Impacts on Harmful Algal Blooms in U.S. Freshwaters: A Screening-Level Assessment
candidates.why_relevant: National-scale, EPA-coauthored screening assessment quantifying projected temperature/climate-driven increases in cyanoHAB likelihood across thousands of U.S. lakes - a key quantitative attribution/projection source.

candidates.title: Climate Change and Freshwater Harmful Algal Blooms
candidates.why_relevant: Authoritative federal (EPA) synthesis of how warming temperatures and heavier precipitation/runoff drive HAB frequency and severity; a citable, official-source anchor for the general climate-HAB link claim.

candidates.title: Impacts of Climate Change on the Occurrence of Harmful Algal Blooms
candidates.why_relevant: EPA fact sheet summarizing climate-driver pathways (temperature, precipitation, CO2, stratification) for HAB occurrence; useful concise, citable federal framing document.

candidates.title: The Role of Climate Change in the Proliferation of Freshwater Harmful Algal Blooms in Inland Water Bodies of the United States
candidates.why_relevant: Recent (2023) AMS-published synthesis specifically on climate change's role in U.S. inland freshwater HAB proliferation, tying meteorological drivers to bloom trends.

candidates.title: Extreme rainfall drives early onset cyanobacterial bloom
candidates.why_relevant: Direct empirical case linking an extreme precipitation event to earlier bloom onset via nutrient pulsing, illustrating the extreme-precipitation-to-nutrient-loading mechanism at event scale.

candidates.title: Climate extremes intensify global lake eutrophication by increasing the stress resistance of harmful bloom-forming algae
candidates.why_relevant: Recent global-scale study showing heatwaves and extreme precipitation (beyond gradual warming) drive bloom dynamics via phosphorus-pulse priming and stress resistance - advances the extreme-events-vs-gradual-warming distinction central to this lane.

candidates.title: Exploring temperature and precipitation impacts on harmful algal blooms across continental U.S. lakes
candidates.why_relevant: Continental-scale statistical analysis directly quantifying how temperature and precipitation variables relate to observed U.S. HAB occurrence, providing empirical grounding for the climate-driver claims.

candidates.title: A REVIEW OF WATER QUALITY RESPONSES TO AIR TEMPERATURE AND PRECIPITATION CHANGES 2: NUTRIENTS, ALGAL BLOOMS, SEDIMENT, PATHOGENS
candidates.why_relevant: Systematic review connecting air temperature and precipitation change to nutrient dynamics and algal bloom response, useful broad mechanistic background for the climate-nutrient-bloom pathway.

candidates.title: Anthropogenic and climatic factors regulate algal bloom intensity and timing in global lakes under climate change
candidates.source_type: Peer-reviewed journal article (Nature Communications Earth & Environment)
candidates.why_relevant: Global-scale disentangling of anthropogenic vs. climatic drivers of bloom timing and intensity, directly relevant to the attribution question (how much is climate vs. nutrient management) central to this lane's uncertainty focus.

candidates.title: Early Seasonal Increases and Persistence in Relative Abundance of Potentially Toxic Cyanobacteria: Concerning Impacts of Extended Ice-Free Periods in Northern Temperate Lakes
candidates.why_relevant: Directly addresses geographic/seasonal range expansion mechanism - warming-driven extended ice-free periods in northern temperate lakes enabling earlier and more persistent toxic cyanobacteria.

candidates.title: Response of cyanobacterial blooms to climate warming: evidence from satellite observations and long-term trends in Lake Taihu in China
candidates.why_relevant: Long-term satellite-based case study of one of the world's most-studied bloom-affected lakes linking observed cyanobacterial trends to climate warming, providing an internationally comparable long-term-trend example outside the U.S.

candidates.title: Comparing MODIS and MERIS spectral shapes for cyanobacterial bloom detection

candidates.title: Two decades of cyanobacterial bloom dynamics in the Great Lakes: insights from multi-mission ocean color sensors
candidates.why_relevant: Presents a 25-year (2000-2024), cross-sensor-consistent CI_cyano time series (MERIS, OLCI, MODIS) for the Great Lakes — directly usable long-term satellite dataset with documented methodology.

candidates.why_relevant: A recent (2025) comprehensive review of cyanoHAB remote sensing trends, including ML-based phycocyanin retrieval, chlorophyll-a, and future algorithm directions — good for establishing state-of-the-art context.

candidates.title: Chlorophyll Algorithms for Ocean Color Sensors — OC4, OC5 and OC6
candidates.why_relevant: NASA's authoritative technical documentation of the standard empirical chlorophyll-a band-ratio algorithms (OC3/OC4 family) used across ocean color sensors, foundational context for chlorophyll-a retrieval before cyanobacteria-specific indices.

candidates.title: The Use of Sentinel-3 Imagery to Monitor Cyanobacterial Blooms
candidates.org_or_authors: (MDPI, Journal of Marine Science and Engineering)
candidates.why_relevant: Peer-reviewed evaluation of Sentinel-3 OLCI band-ratio and index methods specifically for cyanobacterial bloom monitoring — relevant to current-generation (post-MERIS) operational satellite data.

candidates.title: Cyanobacteria Assessment Network (CyAN) | US EPA
candidates.why_relevant: Authoritative primary source for the CyAN program: describes the multi-agency use of Sentinel-3 OLCI (successor to Envisat MERIS) to detect cyanobacteria in U.S. lakes, and is the core sanctioned data source named in the project brief.

candidates.title: A validation of satellite derived cyanobacteria detections with state reported events and recreation advisories across U.S. lakes

candidates.title: Application of Sentinel 3 OLCI for chl-a retrieval over small inland water targets: Successes and challenges

candidates.title: Validation and Comparison of Water Quality Products in Baltic Lakes Using Sentinel-2 MSI and Sentinel-3 OLCI Data
candidates.why_relevant: Head-to-head validation of Sentinel-2 vs Sentinel-3 water-quality retrievals against in-situ lake data, directly informing the spatial-resolution vs revisit-frequency tradeoff central to this lane.

candidates.title: Comparison of iCOR and Rayleigh atmospheric correction methods on Sentinel-3 OLCI images for a shallow eutrophic reservoir
candidates.why_relevant: Directly compares atmospheric correction algorithm choices for OLCI over a shallow eutrophic reservoir, evidencing how atmospheric correction method choice affects inland-water retrieval accuracy.

candidates.why_relevant: Oregon's operational recreational-use-value (RUV) advisory system with public map, explicitly noting most waterbodies are unmonitored — a candid limitation statement useful for framing coverage gaps that remote sensing could fill.

candidates.title: Oregon DEQ Freshwater Cyanobacteria Harmful Algal Blooms (CyanoHABs) Strategy
candidates.why_relevant: State-level strategic framework for prioritizing and responding to freshwater cyanoHABs in Oregon, complementing OHA's advisory-issuance process with the upstream monitoring/assessment side.

candidates.title: NYHABS: New York Harmful Algal Bloom System — Notifications
candidates.org_or_authors: New York State Department of Environmental Conservation (NYSDEC)
candidates.why_relevant: NY's citizen-science-integrated online reporting and interactive mapping system for HAB notifications ('Know it, Avoid it, Report it') — a strong crowdsourced-monitoring model relevant to designing an accessible field tool.

candidates.title: Harmful Algal Bloom (HAB) Program Guide
candidates.org_or_authors: New York State Department of Environmental Conservation (NYSDEC)
candidates.why_relevant: Consolidated technical guide to NY's HAB verification process (visual ID, photo documentation, lab confirmation) and program structure, useful for defining a defensible bloom-confirmation workflow.

candidates.title: U.S. Action Plan for Lake Erie 2018-2023
candidates.org_or_authors: US EPA (Great Lakes National Program Office), in coordination with Ohio, Michigan, Indiana, Pennsylvania, New York
candidates.why_relevant: The binational (with Ontario/Canada) 40%-phosphorus-reduction commitment and monitoring/reporting framework specifically targeting western Lake Erie HABs — essential regional policy context given Lake Erie's centrality to U.S. cyanoHAB research and the brief's regional examples.

candidates.title: About the One Health Harmful Algal Bloom System (OHHABS)
candidates.org_or_authors: US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
candidates.why_relevant: The only national public-health surveillance system linking HAB events to human/animal illness reports (voluntary, state-submitted) — a useful outcome/impact dataset to potentially pair with exposure thresholds, with an honestly-stated non-representativeness caveat.

candidates.title: Welcome to the IOC Harmful Algal Bloom Website (GlobalHAB Programme)
candidates.why_relevant: Primary intergovernmental HAB research program (IOC-UNESCO/SCOR) coordinating global HAB science, observations and modeling including freshwater systems since 2016.

candidates.title: GlobalHAB: A New Program to Promote International Research, Observations, and Modeling of Harmful Algal Blooms in Aquatic Systems
candidates.why_relevant: Peer-reviewed program-defining paper explicitly extending international HAB research scope into freshwater systems, giving scientific framing/rationale for a HAB research agenda.

candidates.title: Harmful Algal Blooms: A Scientific Summary for Policy Makers
candidates.why_relevant: Authoritative UNESCO policy-facing synthesis of HAB science intended to inform management and mitigation decisions, useful for framing product claims to non-scientific stakeholders.

candidates.title: Global Lake Ecological Observatory Network (GLEON) — Working Groups
candidates.why_relevant: International grassroots network of 60+ lake observatories with a dedicated Cyanobacteria Working Group and high-frequency buoy sensor data streams directly relevant to in-situ/weather data fusion.

candidates.title: A Global Lake Ecological Observatory Network (GLEON) for Synthesising High-Frequency Sensor Data for Validation of Deterministic Ecological Models
candidates.why_relevant: Founding paper describing GLEON's high-frequency sensor buoy data model, relevant methodological precedent for combining sensor time series with ecological model validation.

candidates.title: Global Lake Ecological Observatory Network (Cary Institute project page)
candidates.why_relevant: Independent nonprofit ecosystem research institute co-founding/hosting GLEON, developing satellite+drone+buoy fused forecasting methods for cyanobacterial outbreaks.

candidates.title: Cyanobacteria & Algal Blooms (Topic Page)
candidates.why_relevant: Consolidated nonprofit research hub on cyanobacterial bloom drivers and early-warning approaches, useful for background/context and links to primary studies.

candidates.title: Reversal of a Cyanobacterial Bloom in Response to Early Warnings
candidates.org_or_authors: Carey, C.C. et al. (Cary Institute / GLEON-affiliated)
candidates.why_relevant: Directly documents an early-warning system triggering a management intervention that reversed an active cyanobacterial bloom — a concrete precedent for the 'action window' concept in Part B of the tool.

candidates.title: SePRO Corporation — Aquatics Products / SeClear
candidates.why_relevant: SePRO's own flagship algaecide product page describing mechanism (copper-ion photosynthesis inhibition plus phosphorus removal) and timeline (3-7 day control) — ground truth for the case-study sponsor's treatment science.

candidates.title: Solutions for Algae Control (Algae Corner)
candidates.why_relevant: SePRO's own technical/educational content on algae control decision-making, directly informing what 'action' and 'driver' language the tool should mirror for a SePRO field specialist audience.

candidates.title: SePRO Corporation Acquires Earth Science Laboratories, Strengthening Its Water Treatment Portfolio
candidates.why_relevant: Recent (2026) corporate development showing SePRO's expanding water-treatment portfolio (EarthTec algae/taste-odor control) — useful business/product context for the case study's commercial framing.

candidates.title: Stopping Algae Blooms With Bacteria-Busting Buoys
candidates.why_relevant: 2026 ACS press coverage of a novel bacteria-based bloom-control experiment involving SePRO-supplied algaecide, showing current innovation directions adjacent to the case study's treatment angle.

candidates.title: CyanoLakes — Manage Cyanobacterial Blooms Using Satellite Imagery
candidates.why_relevant: Commercial satellite-remote-sensing-only cyanobacteria monitoring subscription service, a direct private-sector analogue/competitor to the CyAN-style signal used in Part A.

candidates.title: BlueGreen Water Technologies — BGi Water Health Intelligence Platform
candidates.why_relevant: Private watertech vendor's 2025 analytics platform explicitly fusing satellite imagery + in-situ sensor + weather data (5+ years) to drive precision treatment decisions — a close commercial analogue to Part A+B of this case study.

candidates.title: EOMAP Water Quality Deep Dive (Remote Sensing Methodology)
candidates.why_relevant: German commercial remote-sensing vendor's physics-based (non-ML) algorithm methodology for chlorophyll/CDOM/turbidity retrieval from optical satellites, an alternative/complementary approach to statistical or ML-based fusion methods.

candidates.title: WHO Guidebook: Toxic Cyanobacteria in Water — 1999 Guideline Values (via EPA summary)
candidates.why_relevant: WHO's original three-tier recreational-water risk thresholds (cell counts / chlorophyll-a) are the internationally recognized baseline that any HAB risk-readout tool's risk categories should be benchmarked against.

candidates.title: WHO/HEP/ECH/WSH/2020.6 — Cyanobacterial Toxins: Microcystins (Background Document)
candidates.why_relevant: Updated (2020) WHO technical background on microcystin drinking-water/recreational guideline derivation, a current authoritative threshold source to cite alongside or in place of the older 1999 values.

lanes.lane: NGO/intergovernmental & private sector — GlobalHAB/IOC-UNESCO, GLEON, academic HAB research centers/foundations, and private-sector vendors (satellite/water-quality analytics, SePRO corporate/science)

candidates.title: Harmful algal blooms in inland waters
candidates.org_or_authors: Feng, Z., Paerl, H.W., et al. (Paerl Lab, UNC)
candidates.source_type: Peer-reviewed review article (Nature Reviews Earth & Environment)
candidates.why_relevant: Authoritative, high-impact review of global cyanoHAB trends (44% rise 2000s-2010s), drivers, and future trajectories from the leading Paerl lab; a foundational synthesis for the review lane.

candidates.title: Harmful algal blooms in inland waters (author PDF copy)
candidates.org_or_authors: Feng, Z., Paerl, H.W., et al.
candidates.why_relevant: Open-access full-text mirror of the Nature Reviews Earth & Environment cyanoHAB synthesis, useful for direct extraction of stated drivers and gaps.

candidates.title: Harmful Cyanobacterial Blooms: Biological Traits, Mechanisms, Risks, and Control Strategies
candidates.org_or_authors: Annual Reviews (Annual Review of Environment and Resources)
candidates.why_relevant: Major synthesis of cyanobacterial bloom biology, toxin mechanisms, ecological risk, and control strategies from a top-tier annual review series — core basic-science reference.

candidates.title: Controlling harmful cyanobacterial blooms in a climatically more extreme world: management options and research needs
candidates.why_relevant: Widely-cited review explicitly framing management options against unresolved research needs under climate extremes — directly names knowledge gaps in bloom control.

candidates.title: Mitigating cyanobacterial harmful algal blooms in aquatic ecosystems impacted by climate change and anthropogenic nutrients
candidates.org_or_authors: Paerl, H.W., Paul, V.J. (Harmful Algae journal)
candidates.why_relevant: Canonical Paerl review linking climate change and nutrient loading to bloom mitigation strategy, foundational to the 'blooms like it hot' framing cited across the field.

candidates.title: Climate change and the aquatic continuum: A cyanobacterial comeback story
candidates.why_relevant: Recent synthesis connecting warming, hydrologic extremes, and cyanobacterial resurgence across the aquatic continuum (streams-to-lakes), useful driver context.

candidates.title: Cyanobacterial blooms. Ecology, prevention, mitigation and control: Editorial to a CYANOCOST Special Issue
candidates.source_type: Peer-reviewed editorial/synthesis of a special issue
candidates.why_relevant: Synthesizes a coordinated European (COST Action) research effort on bloom ecology and mitigation, useful as a cross-study gap-identification summary.

candidates.title: Current approaches to cyanotoxin risk assessment and risk management around the globe
candidates.org_or_authors: Chorus, I. et al. (peer-reviewed; hosted at CDC Stacks)
candidates.why_relevant: Explicitly documents cross-country variation in cyanotoxin thresholds due to incomplete toxicological data and reliance on expert judgment — a direct statement of a regulatory/scientific knowledge gap.

candidates.title: Cyanobacteria Harmful Algae Blooms: Causes, Impacts, and Risk Management
candidates.why_relevant: States current state of knowledge with explicit knowledge-gap language: fragmentary ecotoxicology, contradictions on pH/temperature/virus roles, and unclear human/animal exposure extent.

candidates.title: A Review of Harmful Algal Blooms: Causes, Effects, Monitoring, and Prevention Methods
candidates.why_relevant: Recent (2025) broad review explicitly framed around identifying existing knowledge gaps in HAB research and calling for interdisciplinary prediction/detection approaches.

candidates.title: Expert insights on managing harmful algal blooms
candidates.why_relevant: Directly surveys expert practitioners and names the gap between ecological understanding and human-dimensions/management knowledge, and states forecasting is 'not yet accurate enough at a local scale for widespread use.'

candidates.title: A review on monitoring, forecasting, and early warning of harmful algal bloom
candidates.why_relevant: Focused review of monitoring/forecasting/early-warning methods for HABs; directly relevant to the models-and-methods dimension of the knowledge-gap review lane.

candidates.title: A systematic literature review of forecasting and predictive models of harmful algal blooms in flowing waters
candidates.why_relevant: Systematic review specifically of forecasting/predictive models in flowing (lotic) waters — flags that failure to incorporate uncertainty analysis limits models' practical relevance for management.

candidates.title: Remote sensing identification and model-based prediction of harmful algal blooms in inland waters: Current insights and future perspectives
candidates.why_relevant: Combines remote-sensing detection and model-based prediction review with explicit discussion of current insights vs. future/unresolved perspectives for inland waters.

candidates.title: Application of Optical Remote Sensing in Harmful Algal Blooms in Lakes: A Review
candidates.why_relevant: Recent dedicated review of optical remote-sensing methods for lake HABs; documents spatial/spectral/temporal resolution limitations (e.g., only ~5% of US lakes resolvable at 300m pixel size).

candidates.title: Ground-based remote sensing provides alternative to satellites for monitoring cyanobacteria in small lakes
candidates.why_relevant: Directly demonstrates and quantifies a satellite-detection limitation (small lakes below satellite resolution thresholds) and proposes ground-based alternative — names a specific remote-sensing gap relevant to SePRO's likely small-waterbody customer base.

candidates.title: Remote sensing to detect harmful algal blooms in inland waterbodies
candidates.org_or_authors: ScienceDirect (Science of the Total Environment or similar)
candidates.why_relevant: General review of remote-sensing approaches for inland-water HAB detection, useful for cross-referencing methods and stated constraints.
